skill.name: windows-privilege-escalation
skill.description: Comprehensive Windows privilege escalation techniques for authorized penetration testing and security assessments.
skill.summary: Provides systematic methodologies for discovering and exploiting Windows privilege escalation vulnerabilities during authorized penetration testing engagements.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill contains Windows privilege escalation techniques for authorized penetration testing. Static analysis detected 114 external command patterns (PowerShell, cmd.exe), 9 network references (standard pentest example IPs), and 5 filesystem operations. All findings are contextually appropriate for a defensive security tool. The skill includes proper legal disclaimers and is designed for authorized security testing only.

high_findings.title: Credential Access Techniques
high_findings.description: Skill contains methods for accessing Windows SAM database and Credential Manager for password hash extraction. These are legitimate penetration testing techniques but could be misused.

high_findings.title: Token Impersonation Attacks
high_findings.description: Contains JuicyPotato, PrintSpoofer, and other token impersonation techniques that escalate to SYSTEM privileges. Educational content for authorized testing only.

high_findings.title: Service Exploitation Methods
high_findings.description: Techniques for exploiting misconfigured Windows services including binary path replacement and unquoted service paths. Requires administrative context.

medium_findings.title: Windows Registry Access
medium_findings.description: Contains registry queries for credential discovery and configuration enumeration. Standard Windows administration technique.

medium_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Commands
medium_findings.description: Contains network enumeration commands (netstat, arp, route). Standard penetration testing reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: System Enumeration Commands
low_findings.description: Contains systeminfo, whoami, and other reconnaissance commands. Standard Windows administration and pentesting.

dangerous_patterns.title: PowerShell Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Extensive use of PowerShell for system enumeration, credential access, and exploitation. Contextually appropriate for Windows security tool.

dangerous_patterns.title: Windows Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Uses cmd.exe for command execution and script running. Standard Windows administration pattern.

content.value_statement: Discover and exploit Windows privilege escalation vulnerabilities during authorized penetration testing. Provides systematic methodologies for credential harvesting, service exploitation, and token impersonation.
actual_capabilities: Enumerate Windows system configurations and identify privilege escalation vectors
Extract credentials from SAM database, registry, and stored credential locations
Exploit misconfigured services including unquoted paths and weak permissions
Perform token impersonation attacks using JuicyPotato and PrintSpoofer techniques
Identify and leverage kernel vulnerabilities for privilege escalation
Document findings and generate remediation recommendations
limitations: Requires authorized access to target Windows systems only
Kernel exploits may cause system instability and should be tested carefully
Some techniques require specific Windows versions or configurations
Antivirus and EDR solutions may detect and block common exploitation tools

use_cases.title: Penetration Testing Engagement
use_cases.description: Security consultants performing authorized assessments use this skill to identify privilege escalation paths and demonstrate business impact to clients.

use_cases.title: Red Team Operations
use_cases.description: Red team members leverage these techniques to simulate adversary tactics and test organizational detection and response capabilities.
use_cases.target_user: Red team operators and offensive security teams

use_cases.title: Security Training and CTF
use_cases.description: Security professionals and students use this skill to learn Windows exploitation techniques in controlled lab environments and capture the flag competitions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic System Enumeration
prompt_templates.prompt: Enumerate the current Windows system for privilege escalation vectors. Check user privileges, group memberships, installed software, and running services. Present findings in a prioritized list.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a penetration test with initial user access

prompt_templates.title: Credential Discovery
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for stored credentials on this Windows system. Check the registry, configuration files, Windows Credential Manager, and browser storage. Document any discovered credentials and their potential use.

prompt_templates.title: Service Exploitation Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze Windows services for exploitation opportunities. Check for unquoted service paths, weak service permissions, and vulnerable service configurations. Provide specific exploitation steps for each finding.

prompt_templates.title: Comprehensive Privilege Escalation Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a complete Windows privilege escalation assessment. Run systematic enumeration across all vectors: system info, credentials, services, scheduled tasks, registry, and kernel vulnerabilities. Generate a prioritized exploitation roadmap with specific commands for each path.

output_examples.input: Enumerate privilege escalation vectors on this Windows host
output: - Stored credentials found in Windows Credential Manager

output_examples.input: Find stored credentials on this system
output: DefaultPassword: [REDACTED - use reg query to view]
Key: [Use netsh wlan show profile name=SSID key=clear]
- Test password reuse on other systems

best_practices: Always obtain written authorization before testing any system you do not own
Test exploitation techniques in a lab environment before production use
Document all findings with timestamps and evidence for client reporting
Avoid kernel exploits on production systems due to crash risk
Clean up any tools or files created during the engagement
anti_patterns: Running kernel exploits on production systems without backup and recovery plan
Using loud enumeration techniques that trigger security alerts before objectives are met
Leaving exploitation tools or backdoors on client systems after engagement ends
Testing without proper scope definition and rules of engagement documentation

faq.question: Is this skill legal to use?
faq.answer: This skill is designed for authorized penetration testing only. You must have written permission from system owners before using these techniques. Unauthorized access to computer systems is illegal in most jurisdictions.

faq.question: Will these techniques work on all Windows versions?
faq.answer: No. Techniques are version-dependent. Kernel exploits target specific Windows versions. Always verify OS version and patch level before attempting exploitation. The skill includes version compatibility information for each technique.

faq.question: Why do some exploits fail even when the vulnerability exists?
faq.answer: Antivirus and EDR solutions commonly block known exploitation tools. Try living-off-the-land techniques, custom compiled binaries, or obfuscated payloads. Some exploits also require specific system configurations.

faq.question: What is the safest privilege escalation technique?
faq.answer: Service misconfigurations (unquoted paths, weak permissions) are generally safest as they do not involve kernel code execution. Credential-based escalation using saved passwords is also low-risk compared to kernel exploits.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for defensive security work?
faq.answer: Yes. Understanding offensive techniques is essential for defensive security. Use this skill to audit your own systems, identify vulnerabilities before attackers do, and improve security posture.

faq.question: What tools do I need to transfer to the target system?
faq.answer: Many techniques use built-in Windows commands. For advanced exploitation, common tools include WinPEAS, PowerUp, JuicyPotato, PrintSpoofer, and Mimikatz. Always verify tool hashes and test in lab environments first.
